title: Rapid Prototyping with AI
description: Learn to use Claude to accelerate your prototyping workflow — from generating UI copy to creating interaction flows to building functional prototypes.
learningObjectives: Use Claude to generate diverse design concepts, variations, and alternatives that expand your creative exploration beyond initial ideas
Generate production-quality UI copy and microcopy with Claude, including error messages, onboarding text, and contextual help
Leverage Claude for design critiques that identify usability issues, accessibility gaps, and interaction problems in your prototypes

sections.title: AI-Assisted Design Ideation
sections.content:
Design ideation is the phase where you generate as many concepts, approaches, and variations as possible before narrowing down. Claude is exceptionally good at this because it can produce volume without creative fatigue — and more importantly, it can explore directions that might not occur to you because of your own design biases.

**Generating Concept Variations**

The biggest risk in design ideation isn't bad ideas — it's too few ideas. Designers often converge on a solution too quickly because exploring alternatives is time-consuming. Claude eliminates that bottleneck.

Start with **divergent exploration**: give Claude your design brief and ask for 8-10 fundamentally different approaches to solving the problem. The key here is to ask for genuinely different concepts, not variations of one idea. For example, you might prompt: "I'm designing a feature that helps users organize their bookmarks. Give me 10 different conceptual approaches, ranging from minimal to radical." The goal is to map the solution space before committing to a direction.

Once you have a promising concept, use **constraint-based variations** to stress-test it. Ask Claude how the design would change if you shift one constraint at a time — what if the user is on mobile? What if they're a first-time user? What if they have 1,000 bookmarks instead of 50? What if they're visually impaired? Each constraint shift reveals different design priorities and surfaces assumptions you may not have noticed.

You can also tap into **cross-domain inspiration** by asking Claude to draw analogies from other fields. How do email clients handle message organization? How do music apps handle playlist curation? How do physical filing systems work? Borrowing patterns from adjacent domains often sparks creative solutions that feel fresh precisely because they come from outside your immediate problem space.

**Structured Brainstorming with Claude**

Claude works best in ideation when you give it structured brainstorming frameworks rather than open-ended requests. Three frameworks are particularly effective.

The **SCAMPER method** gives Claude a systematic lens to apply to your design. Ask it to work through each letter: Substitute (what elements could be replaced?), Combine (what features could merge?), Adapt (what patterns from elsewhere could apply?), Modify (what could be larger, smaller, or rearranged?), Put to another use (how else could this feature serve users?), Eliminate (what could be removed entirely?), and Reverse (what if the interaction flow were inverted?). This structured approach prevents Claude from circling around the same ideas.

The **"How Might We" generation** framework bridges research and ideation cleanly. Give Claude your research insights and ask it to generate 10-15 "How Might We" questions. Then pick the most promising ones and ask Claude to brainstorm solutions for each. This two-step process keeps ideation grounded in real user needs rather than drifting into abstract creativity.

Finally, try **worst-case ideation** — ask Claude to generate deliberately terrible solutions to your design problem, then ask it to identify what makes each solution terrible. Often, the inverse of a terrible idea is an interesting one that you wouldn't have considered otherwise. This approach is especially useful when you feel stuck in conventional thinking.

**Using AI Ideation Effectively**

Claude generates volume, but volume without curation is noise. The most productive workflow follows a three-phase rhythm: **generate, then curate, then refine.** Use Claude for the divergent phase where it produces lots of ideas. Apply your own design judgment for the convergent phase where you select the best ones. Then return to Claude for the refinement phase where it develops the selected ideas further.

It's also important to **push past the obvious.** Claude's first suggestions will often be conventional. The interesting ideas usually appear after you say "These are too predictable. Give me 5 more ideas that are unconventional, surprising, or even slightly uncomfortable." Claude responds well to creative provocation, and the later suggestions tend to be more distinctive.

Once you have a rich set of concepts, ask Claude to **combine the best elements** from different ideas into hybrid approaches. You might say: "Take the simplicity of concept 3, the visual metaphor from concept 7, and the interaction model from concept 1. How would those combine?" These mashups often produce the strongest final directions because they cherry-pick the most compelling qualities from across the solution space.

adaptedExamples.designer: When starting a new onboarding flow, ask Claude for 10 fundamentally different approaches — from single-screen wizards to gamified tutorials — then use constraint-based variations to stress-test your top pick across mobile, desktop, and accessibility scenarios before opening Figma.

sections.title: Generating UI Copy and Microcopy
sections.content:
UI copy is the text that users read inside your product — button labels, form instructions, error messages, success confirmations, onboarding text, tooltips, and empty states. Despite being small in size, microcopy has an outsized impact on usability. Claude can generate, iterate, and refine UI copy much faster than writing it from scratch, and it's remarkably good at matching tone and voice guidelines.

**Error Messages That Actually Help**

Error messages are where most products fail users most visibly. Claude can generate error copy that follows best practices, starting with the most important principle: **structure every error message as "What happened + Why + What to do."** Instead of a cryptic "Invalid input," this structure produces messages like "This email address doesn't look right. Make sure it includes an @ symbol and a domain (like name@company.com)." Just specify the technical error and let Claude humanize it.

**Tone calibration** is equally important. Give Claude your brand voice guidelines and ask it to write the same error message in three tones: formal, friendly, and minimal. Then choose the version that matches your product's personality. "Hmm, that password is too short" suits a casual app, while "Password must be at least 8 characters" suits a banking platform. Seeing the same message in different registers helps you find the right voice.

For efficiency, **generate error copy in batches.** List all your error states — form validation, API errors, permission errors, timeout errors — and ask Claude to write copy for all of them at once. This ensures consistency across the entire error experience. When error messages are written one at a time over weeks of development, they tend to drift in tone, structure, and level of detail. Batch generation keeps everything uniform.

**Onboarding and Instructional Copy**

Onboarding copy guides new users through their first experience and is often the most underinvested copy in a product. Claude helps you write it thoughtfully instead of treating it as an afterthought.

For multi-step flows, ask Claude to write **progressive disclosure copy** where each step explains what's happening, tells the user what to do, and previews what comes next. A well-crafted step might read: "Step 2 of 4: Choose your team. Add the people you work with most — you can always add more later. Next, we'll help you set up your first project." This pattern keeps users oriented and reduces the anxiety of not knowing how much is left.

**Empty states** — screens where no data exists yet — are opportunities to educate and motivate rather than leave users staring at a blank page. Ask Claude to generate empty state copy that tells users what they'll see when data exists and what action to take now. Something like "No projects yet. Create your first project to start organizing your team's work" is far more useful than an empty table.

For complex features, layer your help by asking Claude to write short **tooltips** (under 20 words) that explain what a feature does without jargon, along with longer **help text** (2-3 sentences) for users who need more detail. This layered approach matches the copy to the user's level of need — most people just need the tooltip, while a few will want the deeper explanation.

**Generating Variations for Testing**

One of Claude's biggest advantages in copywriting is its ability to generate many variations quickly, which makes it an ideal partner for A/B testing preparation.

When preparing **A/B test candidates**, ask Claude to generate 3-5 variations of a CTA button, headline, or value proposition. Specify the dimension you want to vary: tone (urgent vs. calm), length (short vs. descriptive), or framing (benefit-focused vs. action-focused). These serve as strong starting points for testing rather than shipping the first version and hoping for the best.

If your copy will be translated, Claude can help you write **localization-ready text.** Ask it to flag idioms, cultural references, or wordplay that might not translate well, and request simpler alternatives that convey the same meaning across cultures. Catching these issues early saves expensive rounds of translation revision.

Finally, ask Claude to review your copy for **accessibility.** Labels should make sense when read aloud and out of visual context by a screen reader. "Click here" means nothing without its surrounding visuals, while "Download your invoice" communicates clearly regardless of how it's consumed. This kind of review takes seconds with Claude but significantly improves the experience for users who rely on assistive technology.

playground.description: Try generating UI copy variations for a real design scenario. See how quickly Claude can produce multiple options across different tones and contexts.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a senior UX writer specializing in product microcopy. You write concise, helpful, and tone-appropriate UI text. When given a design scenario, you produce multiple copy variations that follow the 'What happened + Why + What to do' pattern for errors, use progressive disclosure for instructions, and always consider accessibility. Keep all copy scannable and jargon-free.
playground.userMessage:
I'm designing a file upload component for a design collaboration tool. Generate the complete microcopy set:

1. The upload button label and helper text (drag-and-drop area)
2. Upload in-progress state text
3. Three error variations: file too large (limit 25MB), unsupported format (only .fig, .sketch, .pdf, .png), and upload failed due to network
4. Success confirmation message
5. Write each error in two tones: professional (for enterprise) and casual (for creative teams)

Make every message actionable — tell users exactly what to do next.

adaptedExamples.designer: Batch-generate all the microcopy for a file upload component at once — button labels, drag-and-drop instructions, error messages for size limits and wrong formats, and success confirmations — in both enterprise and casual tones so you can quickly A/B test which voice fits your product.

sections.title: Creating Interaction Flows and User Stories
sections.content:
Interaction flows document how users move through your product — the screens they see, the actions they take, and the decisions they make. User stories capture what users need to accomplish and why. Both are essential prototyping artifacts, and Claude accelerates their creation significantly.

**Generating User Stories**

User stories translate user needs into actionable development requirements. The standard format — "As a [user type], I want to [action] so that [benefit]" — is a great starting point with Claude. Provide the feature description and any user research context, and Claude will generate stories that cover the primary use case plus edge cases you might not have considered.

Each user story becomes more useful when paired with **acceptance criteria** — specific, testable conditions that must be true for the story to be considered complete. Ask Claude to generate 3-5 acceptance criteria for each story. For example: "Given the user is on the dashboard, when they click 'Export,' then a CSV file downloads containing all visible data with the current date in the filename." These criteria eliminate ambiguity about what "done" means.

After generating the main stories, turn your attention to **edge cases.** Ask Claude: "What user stories are missing? What happens when the data is empty, when the user lacks permissions, when the network is slow, when the user is on a mobile device?" These edge case stories prevent surprises during implementation, and Claude is particularly good at surfacing scenarios that teams overlook because they're focused on the happy path.

**Mapping Interaction Flows**

Interaction flows are step-by-step descriptions of how users move through a feature, and the best approach is to build them in layers.

Begin with the **happy path** — the ideal flow where the user enters the feature, takes the expected actions, and achieves their goal. Be specific about each screen, interaction, and transition. For instance: "User clicks 'New Project' > Modal appears with project name field and template selector > User types name and selects template > User clicks 'Create' > Dashboard updates with new project card > Success toast appears." This concrete level of detail is what makes a flow useful for prototyping.

Next, identify **branch points** at each step. Ask Claude: "What if the user changes their mind? What if the template they want isn't available? What if they navigate away mid-creation?" Each branch creates a fork in the flow that needs its own design solution. These branches are where the real complexity of a feature lives, and discovering them on paper is far cheaper than discovering them in code.

Finally, map the **error paths.** For each interaction, ask Claude to identify what can go wrong and describe the resulting flow. What if the project name already exists? What if the API call fails? What if the user's session expires during creation? Error paths often constitute the majority of a flow's total complexity, yet they're the part most likely to be designed on the fly during development if you don't plan them upfront.

**Using Claude for Flow Validation**

Before building prototypes, validate your flows with Claude using established evaluation techniques.

A **heuristic evaluation** tests your flow against Jakob Nielsen's usability heuristics. Describe the flow to Claude and ask: "Does this flow provide adequate feedback? Is the system status visible? Can users easily undo their actions?" Claude identifies heuristic violations you might have overlooked because you're too close to the design.

A **cognitive walkthrough** takes a different angle. Ask Claude to role-play as a first-time user encountering the flow. At each step, it should describe: What are you trying to do? Can you see how to do it? After you do it, do you understand what happened? This technique reveals assumptions your design makes about user knowledge — assumptions that are invisible to you as the designer but painfully obvious to a new user.

For a final stress test, ask Claude to find the **weakest point** in your flow — the single step where the most users would get confused, drop off, or make errors. Then iterate on that specific step before investing effort in the rest. This targeted approach gives you the highest usability improvement for the least design effort.

playground.description: Practice mapping a complete interaction flow for a common design pattern. See how Claude helps you think through happy paths, branches, and error states before you open your design tool.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a senior interaction designer who specializes in mapping user flows and writing user stories. When given a feature description, you produce detailed step-by-step interaction flows that cover the happy path, branch points, and error states. You format flows as numbered sequences with clear screen-by-screen transitions. You write user stories in standard format with acceptance criteria. You always consider edge cases and accessibility.
playground.userMessage:
I'm designing a "Save to Collection" feature for a design inspiration app (similar to Pinterest or Dribbble). Users can save any design they're viewing into one or more personal collections.

Map the complete interaction flow:
1. Happy path: User saves a design to an existing collection
2. Branch: User wants to create a new collection during the save action
3. Branch: User wants to save to multiple collections at once
4. Error paths: What happens if the save fails, if the collection name already exists, or if the user hits a storage limit?

For each path, describe the exact screens, UI elements, transitions, and copy the user encounters at every step.

adaptedExamples.designer: Before prototyping a "Save to Collection" feature, ask Claude to map the happy path, branching flows (creating a new collection mid-save, multi-select), and every error state (name collision, storage limit). Discovering these edge cases in text costs minutes instead of hours in your design tool.

sections.title: Using Claude for Design Critiques
sections.content:
Design critique is the process of systematically evaluating a design against criteria like usability, accessibility, consistency, and user needs. Claude can serve as an always-available, never-biased critique partner — it doesn't have organizational politics, doesn't avoid conflict, and doesn't run out of energy.

**Structured Critique Frameworks**

The quality of Claude's critique depends entirely on the framework you give it. Without structure, you'll get vague, surface-level feedback. With the right framework, you'll get specific, actionable insights.

A **heuristic evaluation** is the most common starting point. Describe your design to Claude and ask it to evaluate against Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics. For each heuristic, ask Claude to identify specific violations and suggest concrete improvements. The key to getting useful results is being thorough in your description — include layout, labels, interactions, and error states. Claude can only critique what you describe, so spare no detail.

An **accessibility audit** focuses specifically on inclusivity. Ask Claude to evaluate your design for WCAG compliance by describing your color choices, font sizes, interactive elements, and navigation patterns. Claude can identify issues like insufficient contrast ratios, missing alt text descriptions, keyboard navigation gaps, and focus management problems. These are issues that affect real users but are easy to overlook when you're focused on visual polish.

A **consistency check** examines coherence across your product. Describe multiple screens or components and ask Claude to identify inconsistencies in terminology, spacing, button styles, or interaction patterns. For example, you might discover that on the settings page the save action is a blue button labeled "Save Changes," while on the profile page it's a green button labeled "Update." These small inconsistencies erode user confidence and make your product feel less professional than it is.

**Getting Useful Critiques**

Claude defaults to polite, balanced feedback. That's pleasant to read but not particularly useful for improving your designs. Push it toward more actionable critique with a few techniques.

First, **ask for the top 3 problems** instead of general feedback. This forces prioritization and prevents a laundry list of minor issues that diffuses your attention. Severity-ranked issues give you a clear action plan.

Second, **request specific improvement suggestions.** Don't accept vague feedback like "the navigation could be improved." Ask Claude to describe exactly what it would change and why. A good critique looks like: "Replace the hamburger menu with a persistent bottom navigation bar showing the four primary sections. Rationale: hamburger menus hide primary navigation behind a tap, increasing the effort to switch between sections." This level of specificity turns critique into design direction.

Third, **use multiple critique lenses** by running the same design through several frameworks — usability, accessibility, emotional design, information architecture, and performance. Each lens reveals different issues. A design that passes usability heuristics might fail an accessibility audit, and one that's accessible might still feel emotionally flat. The intersection of these perspectives gives you the most complete picture.

**Iterative Critique Cycles**

The most productive use of Claude for critique is iterative refinement, not one-shot evaluation.

Follow the **describe, critique, revise, re-critique** cycle. Start by describing your design. Get Claude's critique. Describe your revised design, addressing the top issues. Get another round of critique. Repeat until the critique surfaces only minor issues. Each cycle should produce smaller, more focused feedback as the major problems get resolved.

Over time, **track critique patterns** across your designs. If Claude consistently flags the same type of issue — for example, insufficient feedback after user actions — you've identified a systematic gap in your design practice that's worth addressing at the process level, not just the individual design level.

When choosing between approaches, ask Claude to **compare alternatives** directly. Describe two or three design options and ask it to evaluate the tradeoffs of each. For instance: "Option A uses a modal for data entry. Option B uses an inline expansion. Option C navigates to a new page. Compare these three approaches for a user who needs to enter data for 15 items." This comparative critique is faster than building and testing all three options, and it often reveals a clear winner or a hybrid approach that combines the best qualities of each.

adaptedExamples.designer: Describe your notification preferences screen to Claude and ask it to run a heuristic evaluation, then an accessibility audit focusing on screen reader navigation of the toggle grid. Use the prioritized findings to fix the top three usability issues before your next design review.

sections.title: Generate and Refine UI Copy with Claude
sections.content: Practice using Claude to generate production-quality UI copy across different contexts and interaction states.

exercise.prompt:
You're designing a feature that lets users invite team members to a shared workspace. The feature includes an invitation form, email sending, and acceptance flow.

Use Claude as your writing partner to generate the complete copy for this feature:

1. **Invitation form copy** — Write all the UI text for the invite form: the header, the input field labels and placeholders, the helper text, the role selector options (Admin, Editor, Viewer) with brief descriptions of each role, and the send button.

2. **Confirmation and status messages** — Write the copy for: (a) invite sent successfully, (b) invite sent but the email bounced, (c) the invited person already has an account, (d) the user has reached their plan's seat limit.

3. **Invitation email** — Write the subject line and body of the invitation email. It should: identify who sent the invitation, what workspace they're being invited to, what role they'll have, and include a clear call-to-action. Keep it concise — under 100 words for the body.

4. **Acceptance flow** — Write the copy for three screens: (a) landing page when the invitee clicks the email link (what they see before accepting), (b) success state after accepting, (c) expired invitation state with a path forward.

5. **Edge case copy** — Write copy for: (a) a user tries to invite themselves, (b) a user tries to invite someone who was previously removed from the workspace, (c) the inviter's permissions change between sending and the invitee accepting.

For all copy, use a tone that is professional but warm — like a well-run startup, not a corporate enterprise or a casual social app.
hints: Role descriptions should be concise enough to fit in a dropdown but clear enough to prevent users from choosing the wrong role — focus on what each role CAN do rather than what it can't
The bounced email message should help the user solve the problem — suggest checking the email address for typos rather than just reporting the failure
For the invitation email, remember that the recipient may not know what the product is — include enough context for them to understand the invitation without being familiar with the platform
Edge case copy is where your tone is tested most — the 'inviting yourself' message should feel lighthearted, while the 'previously removed' message should be handled carefully and neutrally
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) Complete invitation form copy that includes all specified elements — no missing labels or placeholders. Role descriptions should be concise (under 15 words each) but clear about permissions. (2) Four distinct status messages that follow the 'what happened + what to do next' pattern. The seat limit message should include a path to upgrade, not just report the problem. (3) An invitation email under 100 words that includes all required elements (sender, workspace, role, CTA) and works for recipients who may not know the product. (4) Three acceptance flow screens with appropriate copy for each state — the expired invitation must provide a clear path forward (not just 'sorry'). (5) Three edge case messages with appropriate tone — the self-invite should be lighthearted, and the removed-user message should be neutral and non-judgmental. Deduct points for generic copy that doesn't match the specified tone, for missing elements in any section, or for error messages that only report problems without suggesting solutions.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Apply what you've learned about rapid prototyping with Claude to a realistic design scenario.

exercise.prompt:
You're the lead designer on a new feature: a notification preferences screen where users control which alerts they receive (email, push, in-app) for different event categories (comments, mentions, status changes, weekly digest).

Using Claude as your prototyping partner, complete these three tasks:

1. **Generate the full microcopy set** for this screen — section headings, toggle labels, category descriptions, helper text explaining each notification channel, and a confirmation message when settings are saved. Write all copy in a professional-but-approachable tone.

2. **Map the interaction flow** for a user who arrives at this screen, changes several settings, and saves. Include: the happy path, what happens if the user navigates away with unsaved changes, and what happens if the save fails due to a network error.

3. **Run a mini design critique.** Describe this common pattern to Claude: a simple grid with event categories as rows and channels (email, push, in-app) as columns, with toggles in each cell. Ask Claude to identify the top 3 usability problems with this layout and suggest specific improvements for each.

Share your full Claude conversation showing all three tasks.
hints: For the microcopy, remember that each notification category needs a brief description so users understand what events trigger it — 'Comments' alone is ambiguous without context like 'When someone comments on your files'

The unsaved changes flow is a critical branch point — consider whether to use a modal confirmation, an inline warning, or auto-save, and think about which approach best matches the stakes of the action
When critiquing the grid layout, think about the mobile experience — a table with many columns often breaks on small screens, and toggles can be hard to tap accurately in a dense grid
exercise.evaluationCriteria: A strong response demonstrates three distinct prototyping skills. For microcopy (Task 1): all UI text elements are present and complete — section headings, toggle labels, category descriptions with enough context to disambiguate similar categories, helper text for each channel, and a save confirmation. Copy should be concise, scannable, and consistent in tone. For interaction flow (Task 2): the happy path is described step-by-step with specific screen states and transitions. The unsaved-changes branch should present a clear design decision with rationale. The network error path should follow the 'what happened + what to do' pattern. For design critique (Task 3): the critique should identify real usability issues (not cosmetic preferences) and provide specific, implementable improvements rather than vague suggestions. Look for attention to mobile responsiveness, accessibility (screen reader navigation of a grid), and cognitive load (too many toggles visible at once). The overall response should show the learner using Claude as a thinking partner — iterating on its suggestions, pushing back on generic advice, and applying their own design judgment to Claude's output.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Using Claude for rapid prototyping accelerates every phase of the design process without replacing your judgment.

**Use Claude to expand the solution space.** The biggest ideation risk is converging too early. Claude generates volume without fatigue — use it to explore 10 concepts before committing to one. Push past the obvious first suggestions to find genuinely surprising approaches.

**UI copy is a design material.** Every label, error message, and tooltip shapes the user experience. Claude generates production-quality copy faster than writing from scratch, but always review for tone consistency, brand alignment, and contextual accuracy.

**Document flows before building.** User stories, interaction flows, and edge case maps are cheaper than prototypes. Use Claude to identify gaps, branch points, and error states before you open your design tool.

**Make critique a habit, not an event.** Claude is an always-available, never-judgmental critique partner. Run your designs through structured critique frameworks early and often — the cost of finding problems increases dramatically as you move from concept to prototype to code.
